title: Robot Modeling Control
text:
3D Orientations

Rotation Matrices · Euler Angles · Quaternions







Instructor: Hasan A. Poonawala  Mechanical and Aerospace Engineering  University of Kentucky, Lexington, KY, USA


Topics:  Rotation Matrices (SO(3))  Euler Angles  Quaternions  Comparison & Practical Notes

title: Robot Modeling Control
text:
What Is an Orientation?
A coordinate frame {B}\{B\} attached to a rigid body is described by three orthonormal axes 𝐛1,𝐛2,𝐛3\mathbf{b}_1, \mathbf{b}_2, \mathbf{b}_3 expressed in a fixed world frame {W}\{W\}.

The rotation matrix WRB∈ℝ3×3{}^W R_B \in \mathbb{R}^{3\times 3} stacks those axes as columns:
WRB=[|||𝐛1𝐛2𝐛3|||]{}^W R_B = \begin{bmatrix} | & | & | \\ \mathbf{b}_1 & \mathbf{b}_2 & \mathbf{b}_3 \\ | & | & | \end{bmatrix}





Each column is a unit vector of {B}\{B\} written in {W}\{W\} coordinates. Reading columns gives “where the body axes point.”

title: Robot Modeling Control
text:
The Special Orthogonal Group SO(3)
A rotation matrix satisfies two conditions:

Orthonormality — RTR=I3R^T R = I_3, equivalently R−1=RTR^{-1} = R^T
Proper rotation — det(R)=+1\det(R) = +1


The set of all such matrices forms the Lie group SO(3):
SO(3)={R∈ℝ3×3∣RTR=I,detR=1}\mathrm{SO}(3) = \bigl\{R \in \mathbb{R}^{3\times 3} \mid R^T R = I,\ \det R = 1\bigr\}





SO(3) is a group: closed under multiplication, has identity II, every element has an inverse R−1=RTR^{-1} = R^T, and multiplication is associative.

title: Robot Modeling Control
Robot Modeling Control
text:
Rotating Vectors
If 𝐩B\mathbf{p}^B are the coordinates of a point in frame {B}\{B\}, its coordinates in {W}\{W\} are:
𝐩W=WRB𝐩B\mathbf{p}^W = {}^W R_B\, \mathbf{p}^B

To go the other direction — from world to body — use R−1=RTR^{-1} = R^T:
𝐩B=WRBT𝐩W\mathbf{p}^B = {}^W R_B^T\, \mathbf{p}^W





Active vs. passive interpretation. The same matrix RR can either rotate a vector (active) or change the frame in which it is expressed (passive). We use the passive interpretation: RR maps body coordinates to world coordinates.

title: Robot Modeling Control
text:
Composing Rotations
Two sequential rotations compose by matrix multiplication:
WRC=WRB⋅BRC{}^W R_C = {}^W R_B \cdot {}^B R_C

Order matters — rotation is not commutative:
Rx(α)Rz(β)≠Rz(β)Rx(α)in generalR_x(\alpha)\,R_z(\beta) \neq R_z(\beta)\,R_x(\alpha) \quad \text{in general}





Read the composition right to left: WRC{}^W R_C first applies BRC{}^B R_C (body → intermediate), then WRB{}^W R_B (intermediate → world).

title: Robot Modeling Control
text:
Properties Summary



Property
Expression




Inverse = transpose
R−1=RTR^{-1} = R^T


Determinant
det(R)=+1\det(R) = +1


Columns are orthonormal
RTR=IR^T R = I


Composition
RAC=RABRBCR_{AC} = R_{AB} R_{BC}


Identity
R=I3R = I_3 (no rotation)


9 entries, 3 DOF
6 orthonormality constraints







Rotation matrices have 9 numbers but only 3 degrees of freedom. The 6 orthonormality constraints reduce the effective dimension.

title: Robot Modeling Control
text:
Euler’s Theorem

Euler’s Rotation Theorem: Any orientation of a rigid body can be achieved by a single rotation about some axis through the origin.


A practical consequence: we can parameterize any R∈SO(3)R \in \mathrm{SO}(3) with just three numbers.


Euler angles do this by decomposing RR into three successive rotations about (body or world) axes:
R=Ri(α)Rj(β)Rk(γ),i,j,k∈{x,y,z},i≠j≠iR = R_{i}(\alpha)\, R_{j}(\beta)\, R_{k}(\gamma), \quad i,j,k \in \{x, y, z\},\ i \neq j \neq i

title: Robot Modeling Control
text:
ZYX Convention (Yaw–Pitch–Roll)
The most common convention in aerospace and robotics:
R=Rz(ψ)Ry(θ)Rx(ϕ)R = R_z(\psi)\, R_y(\theta)\, R_x(\phi)



Angle
Symbol
Rotation Axis
Name




1st
ψ\psi
zz
Yaw


2nd
θ\theta
yy
Pitch


3rd
ϕ\phi
xx
Roll




Applied right to left: first roll about xx, then pitch about (new) yy, then yaw about (new) zz.





This is an intrinsic sequence — each rotation is about the body’s current axis. Equivalently, the same result is obtained by extrinsic rotations in the reversed order: Rx(ϕ)R_x(\phi) then Ry(θ)R_y(\theta) then Rz(ψ)R_z(\psi) about fixed world axes.

title: Robot Modeling Control
text:
Expanded ZYX Matrix
Multiplying out Rz(ψ)Ry(θ)Rx(ϕ)R_z(\psi)\,R_y(\theta)\,R_x(\phi):
R=[cψcθcψsθsϕ−sψcϕcψsθcϕ+sψsϕsψcθsψsθsϕ+cψcϕsψsθcϕ−cψsϕ−sθcθsϕcθcϕ]R = \begin{bmatrix}
c_\psi c_\theta & c_\psi s_\theta s_\phi - s_\psi c_\phi & c_\psi s_\theta c_\phi + s_\psi s_\phi \\
s_\psi c_\theta & s_\psi s_\theta s_\phi + c_\psi c_\phi & s_\psi s_\theta c_\phi - c_\psi s_\phi \\
-s_\theta        & c_\theta s_\phi                         & c_\theta c_\phi
\end{bmatrix}
where cα≡cosαc_\alpha \equiv \cos\alpha, sα≡sinαs_\alpha \equiv \sin\alpha.

Extracting angles from RR (when cosθ≠0\cos\theta \neq 0):
θ=arcsin(−R31),ϕ=atan2(R32,R33),ψ=atan2(R21,R11)\theta = \arcsin(-R_{31}), \quad \phi = \mathrm{atan2}(R_{32}, R_{33}), \quad \psi = \mathrm{atan2}(R_{21}, R_{11})

title: Robot Modeling Control
text:
Gimbal Lock
When θ=±90°\theta = \pm 90° (pitch straight up or down), the matrix simplifies and ϕ\phi and ψ\psi become indistinguishable — one degree of freedom is lost.




Gimbal lock is not a physical constraint — the body can still rotate freely. It is a singularity of the parameterization: infinitely many (ϕ,ψ)(\phi, \psi) pairs map to the same RR when θ=±90°\theta = \pm 90°.





Practical consequences:

Numerical instability near θ=±90°\theta = \pm 90°
Cannot smoothly interpolate through singularity
Discontinuous angle extraction

title: Robot Modeling Control
text:
Euler Angle Pros and Cons






Advantages

Minimal — only 3 numbers
Intuitive for human interpretation
Easy to specify end-effector poses
Singularity-free away from gimbal lock



Disadvantages

Gimbal lock at certain configurations
Many conventions (12+ in use)
Not unique (multiple angle sets → same RR)
Poor for interpolation near singularity
Velocity kinematics require a Jacobian

title: Robot Modeling Control
text:
Motivation
Can we find a compact, singularity-free representation?

Axis-angle representation: any rotation is a rotation by angle θ\theta about unit axis 𝐮̂=(ux,uy,uz)T\hat{\mathbf{u}} = (u_x, u_y, u_z)^T, giving the Rodrigues formula:
R=I+sinθ[𝐮̂]×+(1−cosθ)[𝐮̂]×2R = I + \sin\theta\, [\hat{\mathbf{u}}]_\times + (1-\cos\theta)\, [\hat{\mathbf{u}}]_\times^2


where [𝐮̂]×[\hat{\mathbf{u}}]_\times is the skew-symmetric cross-product matrix:
[𝐮̂]×=[0−uzuyuz0−ux−uyux0][\hat{\mathbf{u}}]_\times = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & -u_z & u_y \\ u_z & 0 & -u_x \\ -u_y & u_x & 0 \end{bmatrix}





Axis-angle still has a singularity at θ=0\theta = 0 (axis is undefined). Quaternions resolve this by encoding both axis and angle algebraically.

title: Robot Modeling Control
text:
Unit Quaternions
A quaternion is a 4-tuple:
𝐪=w+x𝐢+y𝐣+z𝐤=(w,𝐯),𝐯=(x,y,z)T\mathbf{q} = w + x\,\mathbf{i} + y\,\mathbf{j} + z\,\mathbf{k} = (w,\, \mathbf{v}), \quad \mathbf{v} = (x,y,z)^T

For rotations we use unit quaternions — those with ∥𝐪∥=1\|\mathbf{q}\| = 1:
w2+x2+y2+z2=1w^2 + x^2 + y^2 + z^2 = 1


Encoding axis-angle (𝐮̂,θ)(\hat{\mathbf{u}}, \theta) as a quaternion:
𝐪=(cosθ2,𝐮̂sinθ2)\mathbf{q} = \left(\cos\frac{\theta}{2},\; \hat{\mathbf{u}}\sin\frac{\theta}{2}\right)





Double cover: 𝐪\mathbf{q} and −𝐪-\mathbf{q} encode the same rotation. Unit quaternions live on S3⊂ℝ4S^3 \subset \mathbb{R}^4, and SO(3) is the quotient S3/{±1}S^3/\{\pm 1\}.

title: Robot Modeling Control
text:
Quaternion Algebra
The imaginary units satisfy 𝐢2=𝐣2=𝐤2=𝐢𝐣𝐤=−1\mathbf{i}^2 = \mathbf{j}^2 = \mathbf{k}^2 = \mathbf{ijk} = -1, giving:
𝐢𝐣=𝐤,𝐣𝐤=𝐢,𝐤𝐢=𝐣\mathbf{i}\mathbf{j} = \mathbf{k},\quad \mathbf{j}\mathbf{k} = \mathbf{i},\quad \mathbf{k}\mathbf{i} = \mathbf{j}

Quaternion product (w1,𝐯1)⊗(w2,𝐯2)(w_1, \mathbf{v}_1) \otimes (w_2, \mathbf{v}_2):
𝐪1⊗𝐪2=(w1w2−𝐯1⋅𝐯2,w1𝐯2+w2𝐯1+𝐯1×𝐯2)\mathbf{q}_1 \otimes \mathbf{q}_2 = \bigl(w_1 w_2 - \mathbf{v}_1\cdot\mathbf{v}_2,\; w_1\mathbf{v}_2 + w_2\mathbf{v}_1 + \mathbf{v}_1 \times \mathbf{v}_2\bigr)


Conjugate and inverse (for unit quaternions):
𝐪*=(w,−𝐯),𝐪−1=𝐪*(since ∥𝐪∥=1)\mathbf{q}^* = (w, -\mathbf{v}), \qquad \mathbf{q}^{-1} = \mathbf{q}^* \quad (\text{since } \|\mathbf{q}\|=1)

title: Robot Modeling Control
text:
Rotating a Vector
To rotate a 3-vector 𝐩\mathbf{p} by quaternion 𝐪\mathbf{q}, embed 𝐩\mathbf{p} as a pure quaternion 𝐩̃=(0,𝐩)\tilde{\mathbf{p}} = (0, \mathbf{p}) and compute:
𝐩̃′=𝐪⊗𝐩̃⊗𝐪*\tilde{\mathbf{p}}' = \mathbf{q} \otimes \tilde{\mathbf{p}} \otimes \mathbf{q}^*

The result 𝐩̃′=(0,𝐩′)\tilde{\mathbf{p}}' = (0, \mathbf{p}') is again a pure quaternion, and 𝐩′\mathbf{p}' is the rotated vector.





This is equivalent to 𝐩′=R𝐩\mathbf{p}' = R\,\mathbf{p} where RR is the rotation matrix corresponding to 𝐪\mathbf{q}. The sandwich product 𝐪(⋅)𝐪*\mathbf{q}(\cdot)\mathbf{q}^* preserves vector length.

title: Robot Modeling Control
text:
Quaternion to Rotation Matrix
Given 𝐪=(w,x,y,z)\mathbf{q} = (w, x, y, z), the equivalent rotation matrix is:
R(𝐪)=[1−2(y2+z2)2(xy−wz)2(xz+wy)2(xy+wz)1−2(x2+z2)2(yz−wx)2(xz−wy)2(yz+wx)1−2(x2+y2)]R(\mathbf{q}) = \begin{bmatrix}
1 - 2(y^2+z^2) & 2(xy - wz) & 2(xz + wy) \\
2(xy + wz) & 1 - 2(x^2+z^2) & 2(yz - wx) \\
2(xz - wy) & 2(yz + wx) & 1 - 2(x^2+y^2)
\end{bmatrix}

This follows directly from expanding 𝐪⊗𝐩̃⊗𝐪*\mathbf{q} \otimes \tilde{\mathbf{p}} \otimes \mathbf{q}^* and collecting terms.

title: Robot Modeling Control
Robot Modeling Control
text:
Spherical Linear Interpolation (SLERP)
Quaternions enable smooth interpolation between two orientations 𝐪0\mathbf{q}_0 and 𝐪1\mathbf{q}_1:
slerp(𝐪0,𝐪1,t)=𝐪0(𝐪0−1⊗𝐪1)t,t∈[0,1]\mathrm{slerp}(\mathbf{q}_0, \mathbf{q}_1, t) = \mathbf{q}_0 \left(\mathbf{q}_0^{-1} \otimes \mathbf{q}_1\right)^t, \quad t \in [0, 1]

Or equivalently, via the angle Ω=arccos(𝐪0⋅𝐪1)\Omega = \arccos(\mathbf{q}_0 \cdot \mathbf{q}_1):
slerp(𝐪0,𝐪1,t)=sin((1−t)Ω)sinΩ𝐪0+sin(tΩ)sinΩ𝐪1\mathrm{slerp}(\mathbf{q}_0, \mathbf{q}_1, t) = \frac{\sin((1-t)\Omega)}{\sin\Omega}\,\mathbf{q}_0 + \frac{\sin(t\Omega)}{\sin\Omega}\,\mathbf{q}_1





SLERP traces a geodesic on S3S^3 — the shortest path between two orientations. Simple linear interpolation of Euler angles does not give the shortest path and can produce unexpected trajectories.

title: Robot Modeling Control
text:
SLERP: Numerical Example
Setup: interpolate from identity to 90° rotation about ẑ\hat{z}, evaluate at t=0.5t = 0.5.
𝐪0=(1,0,0,0),𝐪1=(cos45°,0,0,sin45°)=(22,0,0,22)\mathbf{q}_0 = (1,\;0,\;0,\;0), \qquad \mathbf{q}_1 = \left(\cos 45°,\;0,\;0,\;\sin 45°\right) = \left(\tfrac{\sqrt{2}}{2},\;0,\;0,\;\tfrac{\sqrt{2}}{2}\right)

Step 1 — find Ω\Omega:
cosΩ=𝐪0⋅𝐪1=22⇒Ω=45°\cos\Omega = \mathbf{q}_0 \cdot \mathbf{q}_1 = \tfrac{\sqrt{2}}{2} \;\Longrightarrow\; \Omega = 45°



Ω=45°\Omega = 45° is half the rotation angle (90°). This is a direct consequence of the half-angle encoding w=cos(θ/2)w = \cos(\theta/2).





Step 2 — evaluate at t=0.5t = 0.5:
slerp=sin22.5°sin45°𝐪0+sin22.5°sin45°𝐪1≈0.541(𝐪0+𝐪1)\mathrm{slerp} = \frac{\sin 22.5°}{\sin 45°}\,\mathbf{q}_0 + \frac{\sin 22.5°}{\sin 45°}\,\mathbf{q}_1 \approx 0.541\,(\mathbf{q}_0 + \mathbf{q}_1)
=0.541(1.707,0,0,0.707)≈(0.924,0,0,0.383)= 0.541\,(1.707,\;0,\;0,\;0.707) \approx (0.924,\;0,\;0,\;0.383)

title: Robot Modeling Control
text:
SLERP: Decoding the Result
𝐪0.5≈(0.924⏟w,0,0,0.383⏟vz)\mathbf{q}_{0.5} \approx (\underbrace{0.924}_{w},\;0,\;0,\;\underbrace{0.383}_{v_z})

Decode the rotation angle:
w=cosθ2=0.924=cos22.5°⇒θ=45°w = \cos\!\tfrac{\theta}{2} = 0.924 = \cos 22.5° \;\Longrightarrow\; \theta = 45°
Decode the rotation axis:
𝐮̂=𝐯∥𝐯∥=(0,0,0.383)0.383=ẑ\hat{\mathbf{u}} = \frac{\mathbf{v}}{\|\mathbf{v}\|} = \frac{(0,\;0,\;0.383)}{0.383} = \hat{z}


So 𝐪0.5\mathbf{q}_{0.5} is a 45° rotation about ẑ\hat{z} — exactly halfway between 0° and 90°, on the same axis. ✓



This holds generally: SLERP between two rotations about the same axis stays on that axis and scales the angle linearly. For rotations about different axes, the intermediate axis traces a curved path on S3S^3.

title: Robot Modeling Control
section: SLERP: Where Is the Axis Computed?
text:
SLERP: Where Is the Axis Computed?
In the multiplicative form slerp=𝐪0(𝐪0−1⊗𝐪1)t\mathrm{slerp} = \mathbf{q}_0 \left(\mathbf{q}_0^{-1} \otimes \mathbf{q}_1\right)^t, the axis is explicit:
𝐪rel=𝐪0−1⊗𝐪1←relative rotation from 𝐪0 to 𝐪1\mathbf{q}_{rel} = \mathbf{q}_0^{-1} \otimes \mathbf{q}_1 \quad \leftarrow \text{relative rotation from } \mathbf{q}_0 \text{ to } \mathbf{q}_1
The axis of 𝐪rel\mathbf{q}_{rel} (the normalized vector part) is the axis about which SLERP rotates. Raising to the power tt scales only the angle, leaving the axis unchanged:
𝐪relt corresponds to angle tα about 𝐮̂(𝐪rel encodes angle α about 𝐮̂)\mathbf{q}_{rel}^t \;\text{ corresponds to angle } t\alpha \text{ about } \hat{\mathbf{u}} \quad (\mathbf{q}_{rel} \text{ encodes angle } \alpha \text{ about } \hat{\mathbf{u}})

In our example (𝐪0=\mathbf{q}_0 = identity):
𝐪rel=𝐪0−1⊗𝐪1=𝐪1=(22,0,0,22)⇒𝐮̂=ẑ\mathbf{q}_{rel} = \mathbf{q}_0^{-1} \otimes \mathbf{q}_1 = \mathbf{q}_1 = \left(\tfrac{\sqrt{2}}{2},\;0,\;0,\;\tfrac{\sqrt{2}}{2}\right) \;\Rightarrow\; \hat{\mathbf{u}} = \hat{z}


In the dot-product formula, the axis is not extracted explicitly — it is preserved implicitly because the weighted sum α0𝐪0+α1𝐪1\alpha_0\,\mathbf{q}_0 + \alpha_1\,\mathbf{q}_1 stays on the great circle whose tangent direction encodes 𝐮̂\hat{\mathbf{u}}.

title: Robot Modeling Control
text:
Key Relationships
(ϕ,θ,ψ)⏟Euler ZYX→3 basic rotationsR∈SO(3)⏟rotation matrix→Shepperd’s method𝐪∈S3⏟unit quaternion\underbrace{(\phi, \theta, \psi)}_{\text{Euler ZYX}} \xrightarrow{\text{3 basic rotations}} \underbrace{R \in \mathrm{SO}(3)}_{\text{rotation matrix}} \xrightarrow{\text{Shepperd's method}} \underbrace{\mathbf{q} \in S^3}_{\text{unit quaternion}}

Going backwards:
𝐪→R(𝐪)R→atan2(ϕ,θ,ψ)\mathbf{q} \xrightarrow{R(\mathbf{q})} R \xrightarrow{\mathrm{atan2}} (\phi, \theta, \psi)


The Lie algebra 𝔰𝔬(3)\mathfrak{so}(3) (skew-symmetric matrices) relates to all three via the exponential map:
R=e[𝛚]×,𝐪≈(1,𝛚2) for small ∥𝛚∥R = e^{[\boldsymbol{\omega}]_\times}, \qquad \mathbf{q} \approx \left(1,\, \tfrac{\boldsymbol{\omega}}{2}\right) \text{ for small } \|\boldsymbol{\omega}\|





The vector 𝛚∈ℝ3\boldsymbol{\omega} \in \mathbb{R}^3 is the rotation vector (axis times angle). It is the minimal, globally-defined representation — but it too has a singularity at ∥𝛚∥=2πk\|\boldsymbol{\omega}\| = 2\pi k.

title: Robot Modeling Control
text:
Normal Distribution Transform

A Grid-Based Approach to Scan Matching







Instructor: Hasan A. Poonawala  Mechanical and Aerospace Engineering  University of Kentucky, Lexington, KY, USA


Topics:  NDT Representation  Gaussian Modeling  Optimization-Based Alignment

title: Robot Modeling Control
text:
Lesson Overview

Motivation: Why an alternative to ICP?
NDT Representation: Discretizing space into Gaussian cells
Alignment Algorithm: Optimization over continuous transformation
Comparison: NDT vs ICP trade-offs


Key insight: Represent the target as a distribution, not discrete points

title: Robot Modeling Control
text:
Limitations of ICP
Recall ICP alternates between:

Finding nearest neighbors (discrete correspondences)
Solving for transformation


Problems:

Nearest neighbor is expensive: O(nm)O(nm) or O(nlogm)O(n \log m)
Correspondences change discontinuously
Cost function is not smooth

title: Robot Modeling Control
text:
The NDT Idea
Instead of point-to-point matching:

Convert the target point cloud into a probability density



Divide space into cells (grid)
Fit a Gaussian distribution to points in each cell
Measure how well transformed source points fit the Gaussians



Result: Locally smooth cost function amenable to gradient-based optimization

title: Robot Modeling Control
text:
Step 1: Discretize Space
Overlay a regular grid on the target point cloud:


Code
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from matplotlib.patches import Rectangle

# Generate target points (room-like environment)

wall2 = np.vstack([np.zeros(25), np.linspace(0, 3, 25)])

title: Robot Modeling Control
text:
Step 2: Compute Cell Statistics
For each cell kk containing points {p1,p2,…,pn}\{p_1, p_2, \ldots, p_n\}:

Mean (centroid): μk=1n∑i=1npi
\mu_k = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i



Covariance matrix: Σk=1n−1∑i=1n(pi−μk)(pi−μk)T
\Sigma_k = \frac{1}{n-1} \sum_{i=1}^{n} (p_i - \mu_k)(p_i - \mu_k)^T



Each occupied cell now has a 2D Gaussian: 𝒩(μk,Σk)\mathcal{N}(\mu_k, \Sigma_k)

title: Robot Modeling Control
text:
Visualizing the NDT


Code
from matplotlib.patches import Ellipse

def compute_ndt(points, cell_size, x_range, y_range):
    """Compute NDT representation of point cloud."""
    cells = {}

    for i in range(points.shape[1]):
        x, y = points[:, i]
        # Determine cell indices
        cx = int(np.floor((x - x_range[0]) / cell_size))
        cy = int(np.floor((y - y_range[0]) / cell_size))
        key = (cx, cy)

        if key not in cells:
            cells[key] = []
        cells[key].append(points[:, i])

    # Compute statistics for each cell
    ndt = {}
    for key, pts in cells.items():
        pts = np.array(pts).T  # 2 x n
        if pts.shape[1] >= 3:  # Need at least 3 points for covariance
            mean = pts.mean(axis=1)
            centered = pts - mean.reshape(2, 1)
            cov = (centered @ centered.T) / (pts.shape[1] - 1)
            # Add small regularization for numerical stability
            cov += 0.001 * np.eye(2)
            ndt[key] = {'mean': mean, 'cov': cov, 'n_points': pts.shape[1]}

    return ndt

def draw_ellipse(ax, mean, cov, n_std=2, **kwargs):
    """Draw covariance ellipse."""
    eigenvalues, eigenvectors = np.linalg.eigh(cov)
    angle = np.degrees(np.arctan2(eigenvectors[1, 0], eigenvectors[0, 0]))
    width, height = 2 * n_std * np.sqrt(eigenvalues)
    ellipse = Ellipse(mean, width, height, angle=angle, **kwargs)
    ax.add_patch(ellipse)

# Compute NDT
cell_size = 0.5
ndt = compute_ndt(target, cell_size, (-0.5, 4.5), (-0.5, 3.5))

fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(12, 5))

# Left: Points with grid
axes[0].scatter(target[0], target[1], c='red', s=20, alpha=0.7)
for x in np.arange(-0.5, 4.5, cell_size):
    axes[0].axvline(x, color='gray', alpha=0.3, linewidth=0.5)
for y in np.arange(-0.5, 3.5, cell_size):
    axes[0].axhline(y, color='gray', alpha=0.3, linewidth=0.5)
axes[0].set_title('Target Point Cloud')
axes[0].set_aspect('equal')

# Right: NDT representation
for key, cell in ndt.items():
    draw_ellipse(axes[1], cell['mean'], cell['cov'], n_std=2,
                 fill=True, alpha=0.3, color='blue', edgecolor='blue')
    axes[1].plot(cell['mean'][0], cell['mean'][1], 'b.', markersize=5)

axes[1].set_xlim(-0.7, 4.7); axes[1].set_ylim(-0.7, 3.7)
axes[1].set_title('NDT Representation (2σ ellipses)')
axes[1].set_aspect('equal')

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

title: Robot Modeling Control
text:
The Gaussian Model
Each cell’s Gaussian defines a probability density:
p(x|μk,Σk)=12π|Σk|1/2exp(−12(x−μk)TΣk−1(x−μk))
p(x | \mu_k, \Sigma_k) = \frac{1}{2\pi|\Sigma_k|^{1/2}} \exp\left( -\frac{1}{2}(x - \mu_k)^T \Sigma_k^{-1} (x - \mu_k) \right)


Interpretation: High probability where points are dense, low where sparse


A transformed source point “scores” high if it lands near a cell’s mean

title: Robot Modeling Control
text:
The NDT Cost Function
Given transformation parameters T=(θ,tx,ty)T = (\theta, t_x, t_y):
L(T)=−∑i=1nlogp(T(pi))
L(T) = -\sum_{i=1}^{n} \log p\left( T(p_i) \right)


Equivalently (dropping constants): L(T)=∑i=1n(T(pi)−μk(i))TΣk(i)−1(T(pi)−μk(i))
L(T) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (T(p_i) - \mu_{k(i)})^T \Sigma_{k(i)}^{-1} (T(p_i) - \mu_{k(i)})

where k(i)k(i) is the cell containing transformed point T(pi)T(p_i)

title: Robot Modeling Control
text:
Cost Function Properties
Smooth within each cell: Quadratic in transformation parameters

Discontinuous at cell boundaries: Cell assignment changes


Solution:

Use larger cells (smoother but less precise)
Or use overlapping grids
Or use soft cell assignment

title: Robot Modeling Control
text:
NDT Algorithm: Pseudocode
Input: Source points P, NDT of target (cells with μ, Σ)
Output: Transformation T = (θ, tx, ty)

Initialize T = (0, 0, 0)
repeat:
    # Transform source points
    P' = apply T to P

    # Compute cost and gradient
    L = 0, ∇L = 0
    for each p' in P':
        cell = find_cell(p')
        if cell is occupied:
            L += Mahalanobis_distance(p', μ_cell, Σ_cell)
            ∇L += gradient_contribution(p', μ_cell, Σ_cell)

    # Update transformation (gradient descent or Newton)
    T = T - α * ∇L  (or Newton step)

until convergence
return T

title: Robot Modeling Control
text:
Computing the Gradient
For a single point pp transformed to p′=R(θ)p+tp' = R(\theta)p + t:
∂L∂θ,∂L∂tx,∂L∂ty
\frac{\partial L}{\partial \theta}, \quad \frac{\partial L}{\partial t_x}, \quad \frac{\partial L}{\partial t_y}


Chain rule: Need derivatives of:

Mahalanobis distance w.r.t. p′p'
Transformed point p′p' w.r.t. parameters (θ,tx,ty)(\theta, t_x, t_y)

title: Robot Modeling Control
text:
Gradient Derivation
Let d=p′−μd = p' - \mu and cost term c=dTΣ−1dc = d^T \Sigma^{-1} d

Gradient w.r.t. p′p': ∂c∂p′=2Σ−1d
\frac{\partial c}{\partial p'} = 2 \Sigma^{-1} d



Jacobian of transformation: ∂p′∂(θ,tx,ty)=[−xsinθ−ycosθ10xcosθ−ysinθ01]T
\frac{\partial p'}{\partial (\theta, t_x, t_y)} = \begin{bmatrix} -x\sin\theta - y\cos\theta & 1 & 0 \\ x\cos\theta - y\sin\theta & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}^T

title: Robot Modeling Control
text:
NDT Implementation

def rotation_matrix(theta):
    c, s = np.cos(theta), np.sin(theta)
    return np.array([[c, -s], [s, c]])

def transform_points(theta, t, points):
    """Apply transformation to points (2 x N)."""
    R = rotation_matrix(theta)
    return R @ points + t.reshape(2, 1)

def ndt_cost_and_gradient(params, source, ndt, cell_size, x_range, y_range):
    """Compute NDT cost and gradient."""
    theta, tx, ty = params
    t = np.array([tx, ty])

    # Transform source
    transformed = transform_points(theta, t, source)

    cost = 0.0
    grad = np.zeros(3)  # [d/dtheta, d/dtx, d/dty]

    for i in range(source.shape[1]):
        p = source[:, i]
        p_trans = transformed[:, i]

        # Find cell
        cx = int(np.floor((p_trans[0] - x_range[0]) / cell_size))
        cy = int(np.floor((p_trans[1] - y_range[0]) / cell_size))
        key = (cx, cy)

        if key in ndt:
            mu = ndt[key]['mean']
            cov_inv = np.linalg.inv(ndt[key]['cov'])

            d = p_trans - mu
            cost += d @ cov_inv @ d

            # Gradient
            grad_p = 2 * cov_inv @ d

            # Jacobian of transformation
            c, s = np.cos(theta), np.sin(theta)
            dp_dtheta = np.array([-p[0]*s - p[1]*c, p[0]*c - p[1]*s])

            grad[0] += grad_p @ dp_dtheta
            grad[1] += grad_p[0]
            grad[2] += grad_p[1]

    return cost, grad

title: Robot Modeling Control
text:
NDT Optimization

def ndt_align(source, ndt, cell_size, x_range, y_range,
              max_iters=100, lr=0.01, tol=1e-6):
    """Align source to target using NDT."""
    params = np.array([0.0, 0.0, 0.0])  # theta, tx, ty
    costs = []

    for _ in range(max_iters):
        cost, grad = ndt_cost_and_gradient(
            params, source, ndt, cell_size, x_range, y_range)
        costs.append(cost)

        # Gradient descent with adaptive step size
        step = lr * grad / (np.linalg.norm(grad) + 1e-8)
        params = params - step

        if len(costs) > 1 and abs(costs[-2] - costs[-1]) < tol:
            break

    return params, costs

title: Robot Modeling Control
text:
NDT Demo

np.random.seed(42)

# Create source (subset of target, transformed)

title: Robot Modeling Control
text:
Multi-Resolution NDT
Strategy: Start coarse, refine progressively


Large cells → rough alignment
Medium cells → better alignment
Small cells → fine alignment



Each stage initializes from previous result

title: Robot Modeling Control
text:
Handling Outliers
Both ICP and NDT are sensitive to outliers

NDT approach: Points in empty cells contribute zero to cost

Acts as implicit outlier rejection
Points without nearby target structure are ignored



Additional strategies:

Threshold Mahalanobis distance
Use robust cost functions (Huber, Cauchy)

title: Robot Modeling Control
text:
Practical Considerations
Grid alignment matters:

Axis-aligned grids can miss diagonal features
Solution: Use multiple rotated grids or octrees


Numerical stability:

Covariance matrices can be singular (collinear points)
Add small regularization: Σ←Σ+ϵI\Sigma \leftarrow \Sigma + \epsilon I



Boundary effects:

Points near cell boundaries experience discontinuities
Solution: Overlapping grids or interpolation

title: Robot Modeling Control
text:
Summary

NDT representation: Grid of Gaussians captures local structure
Smooth cost function: Enables gradient-based optimization
Fast alignment: O(n)O(n) per iteration vs O(nlogm)O(n \log m) for ICP
Cell size trade-off: Smoothness vs precision
Multi-resolution: Coarse-to-fine for robustness


NDT is particularly useful for real-time applications with large point clouds
